source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
section: Assess and Classify the Sick Child Aged 2 Months Up to 5 Years — General Danger Signs
text: Any sick child aged 2 months up to 5 years with any one of the following general danger signs is severely ill: the child is not able to drink or breastfeed; the child vomits everything; the child has had convulsions; the child is lethargic or unconscious; the child is convulsing now. These children require URGENT referral to hospital. Give pre-referral treatment (first dose of intramuscular antibiotic, treatment to prevent low blood sugar, and management of convulsions) before transport. Keep the child warm. Refer urgently.

source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
section: Cough or Difficult Breathing — Classification (2 months–5 years)
text: Classify a child with cough or difficult breathing as SEVERE PNEUMONIA OR VERY SEVERE DISEASE if there is any general danger sign, chest indrawing, or stridor in a calm child — give first dose of antibiotic and refer URGENTLY to hospital. Classify as PNEUMONIA if there is fast breathing — give oral amoxicillin for 5 days and follow up in 3 days. Fast breathing thresholds: under 2 months ≥ 60 breaths/minute; 2–12 months ≥ 50 breaths/minute; 12 months–5 years ≥ 40 breaths/minute. If no signs of pneumonia or severe disease, classify as COUGH OR COLD: give home care and return if breathing becomes difficult or fast.

source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
text: Two of the following signs classify the child as having SEVERE DEHYDRATION: lethargic or unconscious; sunken eyes; not able to drink or drinking poorly; skin pinch goes back very slowly (longer than 2 seconds). Refer URGENTLY with the mother giving frequent sips of ORS on the way. Two of: restless and irritable; sunken eyes; drinks eagerly, thirsty; skin pinch goes back slowly — classify as SOME DEHYDRATION (Plan B: give ORS, zinc, and reassess in 4 hours). Not enough signs to classify as some or severe dehydration — NO DEHYDRATION (Plan A: home care with ORS and zinc).

source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
section: Fever — Classification when Malaria Risk is High (2 months–5 years)
text: A child with fever and any general danger sign or stiff neck is classified as VERY SEVERE FEBRILE DISEASE. Give the first dose of an appropriate antibiotic, give a dose of artesunate (or other parenteral antimalarial in malaria-risk areas), prevent low blood sugar, give paracetamol for high fever (≥ 38.5°C), and refer URGENTLY to hospital. In a high malaria-risk area, fever (by history or temperature ≥ 37.5°C) without general danger signs or stiff neck is classified as MALARIA and treated with first-line antimalarial per national policy.

source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
text: Classify a child with measles (or measles in the last 3 months) as SEVERE COMPLICATED MEASLES if any general danger sign is present, or clouding of the cornea, or deep or extensive mouth ulcers. Refer URGENTLY to hospital with the first dose of vitamin A and first dose of antibiotic. MEASLES WITH EYE OR MOUTH COMPLICATIONS — give vitamin A, treat eye infection with tetracycline eye ointment, treat mouth ulcers with gentian violet, follow up in 3 days.

source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
text: Tender swelling behind the ear classifies as MASTOIDITIS — give first dose of antibiotic, give first dose of paracetamol for pain, refer URGENTLY to hospital. Pus seen draining from the ear and discharge reported for less than 14 days OR ear pain classifies as ACUTE EAR INFECTION — give 5 days of oral amoxicillin, dry the ear by wicking, follow up in 5 days. Pus draining for 14 days or more classifies as CHRONIC EAR INFECTION — dry the ear by wicking and treat with topical quinolone ear drops; oral antibiotics are not effective.

source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
section: Check for Acute Malnutrition (2 months–5 years)
text: If a child has visible severe wasting OR oedema of both feet OR mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) less than 115 mm, classify as COMPLICATED SEVERE ACUTE MALNUTRITION when any IMCI general danger sign or medical complication is present — give first dose of antibiotic and refer URGENTLY to hospital. If no complications, classify as UNCOMPLICATED SEVERE ACUTE MALNUTRITION — give ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF), routine medications per national protocol, and follow up weekly.

source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
section: Young Infant 1 Week up to 2 Months — Possible Serious Bacterial Infection or Very Severe Disease
text: A young infant aged 1 week up to 2 months with any of the following signs has POSSIBLE SERIOUS BACTERIAL INFECTION OR VERY SEVERE DISEASE and must be referred urgently: not feeding well; convulsions; fast breathing (≥ 60 breaths/minute); severe chest indrawing; fever (38°C or above) or low body temperature (below 35.5°C); movements only when stimulated or no movements at all; bulging fontanelle; pus draining from the umbilicus with redness extending to the skin; severe jaundice (yellow palms or soles). Give first dose of intramuscular antibiotic and refer URGENTLY.

source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
section: Young Infant — Feeding Problem or Low Weight for Age
text: A young infant is classified as having FEEDING PROBLEM OR LOW WEIGHT if any of: not well attached to the breast or not suckling effectively; less than 8 breastfeeds in 24 hours; receiving other foods or drinks; low weight for age; thrush (ulcers or white patches in mouth). Advise the mother to breastfeed as often and for as long as the infant wants, day and night. Teach correct positioning and attachment. If receiving other foods or drinks, counsel about breastfeeding more frequently. Follow up in 2 days for any feeding problem.

source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
text: A child with severe palmar pallor is classified as SEVERE ANAEMIA — refer URGENTLY to hospital. Some palmar pallor classifies as ANAEMIA — give iron, mebendazole if older than 1 year and not given in last 6 months, and follow up in 14 days. If no palmar pallor, no anaemia. Children living in hookworm or whipworm endemic areas should receive routine deworming every 6 months from age 1 year.

source: India MoHFW Standard Treatment Guidelines — Cardiology
section: Acute Coronary Syndrome — Initial Recognition and Pre-Hospital Care
text: Suspect acute coronary syndrome (ACS) in any patient with central crushing or pressing chest pain (often described as a heavy weight) lasting more than 20 minutes, especially if associated with one or more of the following: radiation to the left arm, jaw, neck, or back; profuse sweating; nausea or vomiting; shortness of breath; syncope; or a sense of impending doom. In women, diabetics, and the elderly the presentation may be atypical with epigastric or jaw discomfort, breathlessness, fatigue, or syncope without classic chest pain. Refer immediately to a facility with cardiac monitoring and reperfusion capability; give chewable aspirin 300 mg unless contraindicated.

source: India MoHFW Standard Treatment Guidelines — Cardiology
section: Chest Pain Red Flags Requiring Immediate Transfer
text: Red-flag features in a patient with chest pain that mandate immediate transfer to an emergency facility: pain lasting more than 20 minutes; pain radiating to arm, jaw, or back; associated diaphoresis, dyspnoea, syncope, or nausea; haemodynamic instability (systolic BP < 90 mmHg, HR > 110 or < 40); known coronary artery disease, diabetes mellitus, or hypertension with new chest discomfort; new ECG changes (ST elevation, depression, T-wave inversion). Time to reperfusion is the single most important determinant of myocardial salvage — every 30 minutes of delay reduces survival benefit.

source: WHO Package of Essential Noncommunicable Disease Interventions (PEN) — Acute Management of ACS
text: For ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), the goal is reperfusion within 90 minutes of first medical contact for primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI), or within 30 minutes for fibrinolytic therapy where PCI is not available. In primary-care settings, the priority is rapid recognition and transfer with concurrent administration of aspirin 300 mg chewed, and where available, clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose. Oxygen is reserved for patients with SpO2 < 90% or in respiratory distress. Sublingual nitroglycerin may be used cautiously for ongoing pain.

source: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summaries — Chest Pain
section: Initial Assessment of Suspected Cardiac Chest Pain
text: Cardiac chest pain is typically described as central or left-sided, heavy or pressing, lasting more than 15 minutes, and may radiate to the arms (particularly the left), jaw, neck, or back. Associated features increasing suspicion include sweating, breathlessness, nausea, vomiting, and a sense of impending doom. Pain reproduced by chest wall palpation or by inspiration is less likely to be cardiac. A 12-lead ECG should be obtained within 10 minutes of presentation. Troponin testing is mandatory if ACS is suspected; a single high-sensitivity troponin within normal range at presentation does not rule out non-ST-elevation MI if symptom onset was within 3 hours.

source: WHO Guidelines on Stroke and Cerebrovascular Disease — Recognition and Pre-Hospital Care
text: The FAST screen is the recommended pre-hospital tool for suspected stroke: Face — ask the person to smile, look for one side of the face drooping; Arms — ask the person to raise both arms, look for one arm drifting down or unable to rise; Speech — ask the person to repeat a simple phrase, listen for slurred or strange speech; Time — note the time symptoms started and call emergency services immediately. Any positive sign requires emergency transport to a stroke-capable centre. The window for intravenous thrombolysis is up to 4.5 hours from symptom onset; mechanical thrombectomy may extend the window to 24 hours in selected patients. Time = brain.

source: India MoHFW Standard Treatment Guidelines — Neurology
text: Recognise stroke in any adult with sudden onset of one or more of the following: unilateral facial droop, arm weakness, or slurred speech (FAST positive); sudden severe headache of maximum intensity ("thunderclap" headache — consider subarachnoid haemorrhage); sudden visual loss in one or both eyes; sudden confusion or altered consciousness; sudden vertigo with imbalance. Note the exact time of symptom onset (or when the patient was last known to be well). Transfer immediately to a centre with CT imaging and stroke unit capability. Do not give aspirin until intracranial haemorrhage is excluded by imaging.

source: WHO Cerebrovascular Disease Reference — Subarachnoid Haemorrhage Recognition
text: A thunderclap headache is a sudden-onset severe headache that reaches maximum intensity within seconds to a minute — often described by the patient as "the worst headache of my life." It must be assumed to be subarachnoid haemorrhage until proven otherwise, even if neurological examination is initially normal. Other features that increase suspicion: vomiting, neck stiffness, photophobia, transient loss of consciousness at onset, focal neurological signs. Urgent non-contrast CT brain is the investigation of choice within 6 hours of onset; lumbar puncture for xanthochromia if CT is negative and clinical suspicion remains.

source: India MoHFW Standard Treatment Guidelines — Respiratory
section: Severe Asthma Exacerbation — Recognition and Immediate Management
text: Recognise a severe or life-threatening asthma exacerbation when any of the following are present: inability to speak in full sentences; respiratory rate ≥ 30/min in adults; heart rate ≥ 120/min; SpO2 < 92% on room air; use of accessory muscles or chest indrawing; silent chest on auscultation; cyanosis; exhaustion or altered consciousness. Immediate treatment: high-flow oxygen to maintain SpO2 94–98%, nebulised salbutamol 5 mg (with ipratropium 0.5 mg in life-threatening cases) repeated every 20 minutes, oral or IV corticosteroid (prednisolone 40–50 mg or hydrocortisone 100 mg IV). A silent chest, exhaustion, or falling SpO2 indicates imminent respiratory failure — transfer to ICU.

source: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children — Pneumonia Severity Assessment
text: Classify as SEVERE PNEUMONIA in a child 2–59 months with cough or difficult breathing AND any of: central cyanosis or SpO2 < 90%; severe respiratory distress (grunting, very severe chest indrawing); signs of pneumonia plus general danger sign (inability to drink, persistent vomiting, convulsions, lethargy or loss of consciousness). Admit and treat with parenteral antibiotics and oxygen. Classify as PNEUMONIA if fast breathing alone — manage with oral amoxicillin as outpatient. Reassess at 48 hours; if worsening, treat as severe.

source: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summaries — Asthma (Adults)
text: Acute asthma is assessed by severity: MODERATE — increasing symptoms, peak expiratory flow (PEF) 50–75% best or predicted, no features of acute severe asthma. ACUTE SEVERE — PEF 33–50% best or predicted, respiratory rate ≥ 25/min, heart rate ≥ 110/min, inability to complete sentences. LIFE-THREATENING — PEF < 33%, SpO2 < 92%, silent chest, cyanosis, poor respiratory effort, arrhythmia, exhaustion, altered consciousness, hypotension. NEAR-FATAL — raised PaCO2 or need for mechanical ventilation. Life-threatening features require immediate transfer with oxygen, nebulised bronchodilators, and systemic steroid.

source: India MoHFW Standard Treatment Guidelines — Obstetrics
text: Vaginal bleeding in the first trimester (up to 12 weeks) requires urgent assessment for ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage. Red-flag features: heavy bleeding (soaking a pad in less than an hour), severe abdominal or shoulder-tip pain, syncope, hypotension (systolic BP < 100), tachycardia (HR > 100), pallor. Suspect ectopic pregnancy in any sexually active woman of reproductive age with abdominal pain and amenorrhoea; up to 50% of ectopics present without classic bleeding. Refer immediately to a facility with surgical capability and blood transfusion services. Do not perform a vaginal examination if placenta praevia is suspected in later pregnancy.

source: WHO Recommendations for Prevention and Treatment of Pre-eclampsia and Eclampsia
text: Severe pre-eclampsia is defined as systolic BP ≥ 160 mmHg or diastolic BP ≥ 110 mmHg on two occasions at least 4 hours apart in a previously normotensive pregnant woman after 20 weeks, with proteinuria; OR new-onset hypertension in pregnancy with any of: persistent severe headache, visual disturbances (blurring, flashing lights, scotoma), persistent right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, oliguria, pulmonary oedema, thrombocytopenia, elevated liver enzymes, fetal growth restriction. Eclampsia (seizure) is an immediate threat. Give magnesium sulphate (loading 4 g IV slow then 1 g/hour infusion) for seizure prophylaxis and refer urgently. Definitive treatment is delivery.

source: India MoHFW Standard Treatment Guidelines — Obstetrics
section: Pregnancy Red Flags Requiring Immediate Assessment
text: Any pregnant woman with any of the following requires immediate referral to a facility with obstetric and surgical capability: vaginal bleeding at any gestation; severe abdominal pain; severe headache with visual changes; convulsions; reduced fetal movements after 28 weeks; rupture of membranes before 37 weeks; signs of shock (pallor, tachycardia, hypotension, altered mental status); high fever (≥ 38°C); persistent vomiting with dehydration. Counsel every pregnant woman about these danger signs at the first antenatal visit and at each subsequent contact.

source: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care — Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding (Adults)
text: Suspect upper gastrointestinal bleeding in any patient with haematemesis (vomiting fresh red blood or coffee-ground material), melena (black tarry stool), or unexplained hypotension with anaemia. Risk-stratify by haemodynamic status: shock (HR > 110, systolic BP < 90, altered mental status, pallor, cool peripheries) indicates active major bleeding — give IV crystalloid resuscitation, two large-bore cannulae, group-and-cross-match blood, and urgent endoscopy. Variceal bleeding is suggested in patients with chronic liver disease (stigmata: spider naevi, palmar erythema, ascites) and warrants empirical terlipressin or octreotide and prophylactic antibiotic before endoscopy.

source: India MoHFW Standard Treatment Guidelines — General Surgery
text: An acute abdomen is severe abdominal pain of sudden or rapidly progressive onset, with one or more of: rigidity, rebound tenderness, guarding, absent bowel sounds, fever, vomiting, distension, or haemodynamic instability. Surgical red flags warranting immediate referral: sudden severe pain reaching maximum within minutes (consider perforation, ruptured aneurysm, ectopic pregnancy); pain with rigidity (peritonitis); pain with shock; pain with melena or haematemesis (consider mesenteric ischaemia, GI bleed); pain in pregnancy at any gestation; pain in an immunocompromised patient. Avoid opioid analgesia until surgical review unless pain is intolerable. Keep the patient nil per oral.

source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014
text: Jaundice in a young infant aged 1 week up to 2 months must be assessed for severity. SEVERE JAUNDICE is present when there is yellow discolouration of the palms and soles, or when jaundice has been present for more than 14 days, or jaundice appeared on day one of life. Severe jaundice requires URGENT referral to hospital for phototherapy and assessment for haemolytic disease or biliary atresia. Jaundice of the face only that appeared after 24 hours of age and resolved by 14 days may be physiological — advise the mother to continue breastfeeding frequently and return immediately if the jaundice spreads to the legs or palms.

source: WHO mhGAP Intervention Guide 2.0 — Self-Harm / Suicide
text: Any person who presents with thoughts, plans, or acts of self-harm or suicide requires a careful clinical assessment of imminent risk. Place the person in a secure and supportive environment; do not leave them alone. Remove access to means of self-harm. Ask directly: "Have you thought about ending your life? Do you have a plan? Do you have the means?" Asking does not increase risk. Mobilise family and social support. Refer urgently to mental health services. For all contacts, provide the telephone numbers of crisis helplines. In India: iCall — 9152987821 (24×7, multilingual including English, Hindi, Kannada); Vandrevala Foundation — 1860-2662-345 (24×7). National emergency number 112; ambulance 108.

source: WHO mhGAP Intervention Guide 2.0 — Acute Stress and Crisis Response
text: Provide psychological first aid to any person in acute distress: ensure safety; listen without pressure to talk; acknowledge their feelings without judgement; provide accurate information about what is happening and what services are available; help connect with social supports, basic services, and ongoing care. Avoid telling the person to forget the event, minimise their distress, or push them to share more than they wish. Specific psychotropic medication is not recommended for acute stress in the first month except for short-term symptom relief of severe insomnia. Follow up within one week, and again within one month.

source: WHO Emergency Triage Assessment and Treatment (ETAT) — General Principles
section: ABCD Approach to the Acutely Ill Patient
text: Every acutely ill patient is assessed in this order: A — Airway (is it open and protected; consider obstruction by foreign body, oedema, secretions); B — Breathing (respiratory rate, effort, SpO2, accessory muscle use, cyanosis); C — Circulation (heart rate, capillary refill, blood pressure, pallor, peripheral temperature); D — Disability (level of consciousness using AVPU — Alert, responds to Voice, responds to Pain, Unresponsive — and pupillary responses). Any abnormality at any step takes priority over subsequent steps. Identification of any emergency sign (obstructed airway, severe respiratory distress, central cyanosis, signs of shock, coma, convulsions, severe dehydration) requires immediate management before further history-taking.

source: WHO Integrated Management of Adolescent and Adult Illness (IMAI) — General Danger Signs in Adults
section: Quick Check Triage for Adults and Adolescents
text: On first contact with any adolescent or adult patient, screen for these emergency signs that require immediate management before history-taking: airway compromise (stridor, difficulty speaking); respiratory distress (severe shortness of breath, SpO2 < 90%, cyanosis); shock (cold extremities, capillary refill > 3 seconds, weak fast pulse, systolic BP < 90); altered mental state (confusion, drowsiness, unresponsive); seizures; severe pain; severe bleeding from any site; suspected pregnancy with bleeding or severe pain; signs of severe dehydration; very high fever (> 39.5°C) with confusion. Move any positive patient to the resuscitation area and call for senior help.
